nativeRange.habitat: Rocky reefs, coral reef slopes, caves, overhangs, and under ledges and pier pilings on hard substrates away from direct sunlight; also colonizes artificial structures (metal, concrete, plastic, rope)

decorPreferences: low-light zones away from direct illumination
stable hard substrate for stolon attachment

diet.notes: Azooxanthellate (non-photosynthetic) filter feeder. In the wild, diet has been documented as approximately 88% phytoplankton (primarily cyanobacteria and diatoms), 7% small zooplankton, and 5% unidentified organic particles. In captivity, daily target feeding with a mix of particle sizes is generally advised: live or preserved phytoplankton, small zooplankton (rotifers, copepods, calanus), and enriched marine snow. Multiple feedings per day tend to support better long-term health given the species' continuous filter-feeding behavior.

goodWith: most invertebrates that do not graze on coral tissue
avoidWith: fast-growing photosynthetic corals that may overgrow NPS colonies
hermit crabs and urchins that may dislodge or trample colonies
strong algae grazers whose disturbance can damage stolon attachments
other photosynthetic corals in close proximity (allelopathy potential)

summary: Carijoa riisei is an azooxanthellate (non-photosynthetic) soft coral that filter-feeds on phytoplankton and zooplankton. Advanced care; requires daily feeding and strong flow. INVASIVE in Hawaii — never release into the ocean.
careNotes:
⚠️ INVASIVE STATUS — CRITICAL: Carijoa riisei is established as an invasive species in Hawaii and parts of the Western Atlantic/Indo-Pacific, considered one of the most ecologically damaging marine invasives in Hawaiian waters. It has been documented overgrowing and killing 70–90% of native black coral (Antipathes) colonies at depths of 65–115 m off Maui. It is also documented in the souvenir and aquarium trades. Aquarists should NEVER release this species, tank water, or associated materials into the ocean or any waterway. Consider the ecological impact carefully before purchasing.

Biology: This octocoral forms erect, branching colonies of hollow, flexible stems reaching 10–36 cm in height, with small white polyps at branch tips bearing eight pinnate tentacles (giving the 'snowflake' appearance). The species lacks symbiotic zooxanthellae and derives no energy from light — it is an obligate filter feeder.

Lighting: Because C. riisei is azooxanthellate, it does not require light and actively thrives in shaded, low-light zones. PAR values of roughly 20–80 are generally sufficient. High light is more detrimental than beneficial, as it promotes growth of competing photosynthetic algae that can overgrow the colony. Placement under overhangs, in the shadow of rockwork, or in shaded areas of the aquarium is strongly recommended.

Flow: Strong, randomized water movement is essential — the species is a passive filter feeder that relies entirely on water current to deliver food particles to its extended polyps. Flow comparable to that used for SPS corals is generally advised, with the polyps visibly swaying in the current.

Placement: Anywhere in the tank with strong flow and low light; under overhangs or on shaded rock faces mimics natural habitat (caves, pier pilings, ledge undersides).

Feeding: Daily feeding is required, and multiple small feedings per day tend to produce the best outcomes given the species' near-continuous filter-feeding behavior in nature. A diverse mix of particle sizes is recommended: live or preserved phytoplankton (small cell size: cyanobacteria, Nannochloropsis, Isochrysis), small zooplankton (rotifers, tigger pods, calanus, baby brine shrimp), and enriched marine snow. The diet in wild populations has been documented as predominantly phytoplankton (~88%) by biomass.

Water parameters: Standard reef chemistry is generally appropriate. The species is limited at lower temperatures by approximately 22–23°C in the wild; captive temperatures of 23–27°C are generally cited. Salinity should be maintained as euhaline (30–35 PSU / SG 1.023–1.026). Standard reef pH, alkalinity, calcium, and magnesium ranges apply.

Care difficulty: This species is generally considered advanced due to its non-photosynthetic feeding requirements. Aquarists must commit to daily feeding regimens and maintain excellent filtration to handle the elevated nutrient load from frequent feeding. Some sources note there have been few documented cases of long-term captive success, though this may reflect limited hobbyist experience with the species rather than inherent impossibility.

Native range note: Although C. riisei was first described from the Caribbean in 1860 and was long considered a Western Atlantic native, a 2010 phylogenetic study concluded the species is actually Indo-West Pacific in origin and invasive in the Atlantic. It is now invasive across much of the Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, and Hawaiian Islands.

Natural predators: Limited natural predation is documented from nudibranchs (Phyllodesmium poindimiei, Tritoniopsis elegans) and bearded fireworms, though these are insufficient for biocontrol purposes.
breedingNotes: Colonies are gonochoristic (separate male and female colonies). Sexual reproduction occurs continuously year-round — an unusual strategy among corals. Colonies typically need to reach 25–54 cm in height before initiating sexual reproduction. Females produce approximately 3.5–7.4 oocytes per polyp depending on location. Planula larvae are produced and settle on hard surfaces, where they develop via creeping stolons. Asexual reproduction occurs readily through fragmentation and stolon extension, which contributes to the species' invasive spread. Deliberate captive breeding is not documented in the hobby.

primary.name: NEMESIS (Smithsonian Environmental Research Center) — Carijoa riisei species summary
primary.notes: Primary biological reference. Used for taxonomy, native range, invasive range, temperature tolerance (min 23°C / limited by 22°C isotherm), salinity tolerance (euhaline 30–40 PSU), depth range, feeding biology (diet composition 88% phytoplankton / 7% zooplankton), colony size (up to 355 mm), and reproductive biology. LiveAquaria (assigned primary) does not currently list this species; NEMESIS used as scientific primary per playbook swap rule.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Carijoa riisei
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Order Malacalcyonacea, Family Clavulariidae), invasive status in Hawaii (black coral impact), native range (Indo-Pacific origin per 2010 phylogenetic study), depth range (0–180 ft / 0–55 m), colony morphology, and predator documentation. Confirmed azooxanthellate / NPS status.

additional.name: IUCN GISD — Carijoa riisei full account
additional.notes: Cross-checked invasive status, colony height (10–25 cm common), habitat (turbid water, strong currents, shade-loving), feeding biology (obligate zooplankton predator/filter feeder), and ecological impact documentation. Also confirmed the species is used in the aquarium trade.

additional.name: Texas Invasive Species Institute — Carijoa riisei
additional.notes: Cross-checked invasive range (Florida, Hawaii, Puerto Rico, Gulf of Mexico), habitat (turbid, organic-rich water), and confirmed still actively sold in aquarium trade. Native range now understood as Indo-Pacific per phylogenetic revision.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org — White Telesto (Carijoa riisei)
additional.notes: Cross-checked colony size (5–25 cm), depth range (0–55 m), geographic distribution (Caribbean, Bahamas, Florida, Indo-Pacific, Hawaii), invasive status confirmation.

additional.name: Pod Your Reef — NPS Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for general NPS coral husbandry parameters: temperature 24–26°C, salinity SG ~1.025, pH 7.9–8.3, alkalinity 7–9 dKH, calcium ~420 ppm, magnesium ~1300 ppm, feeding frequency (daily, 2–3x/day optimal), flow (strong, randomized), lighting (low, detrimental if excessive). Carijoa riisei not specifically mentioned.
